title: NEC (Necrotising Enterocolitis)
oneLiner: Life-threatening intestinal necrosis in preterm neonates (<32w, <1500g); rapid progression to perforation, peritonitis, septic shock.

sections.Necrotising Enterocolitis (NEC): **One-liner:** Life-threatening intestinal necrosis in preterm neonates (<32w, <1500g); rapid progression to perforation, peritonitis, septic shock.
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Intestinal perforation** → pneumoperitoneum on AXR (free air under diaphragm) + board-like abdomen, septic shock. **Single finding:** abdominal wall erythema and absent bowel sounds with haemodynamic instability.
- **Septic shock due to translocation** → rising lactate, hypotension, mottling, apnoea. **Single finding:** lactate >4 mmol/L despite fluid bolus.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** abdominal distension, feeding intolerance (vomiting, increased gastric residuals), bloody stools, lethargy, temperature instability, apnoea, bradycardia.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Distended, tense, erythematous abdomen
  - Reduced/absent bowel sounds
  - Palpable abdominal mass (in advanced cases)
  - Signs of shock: mottled skin, delayed cap refill, hypotension (late)
  - Perforation: board-like rigidity, extreme tenderness
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Sepsis (non-GI focus)** → positive blood cultures from other source; no bloody stools, normal AXR
- **Spontaneous intestinal perforation (SIP)** → isolated perforation without pneumatosis; more common in ELBW <26w; no pneumatosis on AXR
- **Feeding intolerance (non-NEC)** → mild distension, stable observations, no bloody stools, normal AXR
- **Malrotation with volvulus** → bilious vomiting, distended abdomen, but bloody stools rare; UGI confirms
- **Infectious enteritis** → frequent watery/bloody stools, no pneumatosis on AXR; fever may be present
- **Splitter:** **Pneumatosis intestinalis on AXR** = NEC (not SIP, not simple feeding intolerance)
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - NG tube for decompression + aspirate check
  - Rectal exam for occult blood
  - Observations: HR, RR, SpO2, BP, temperature
  - Abdominal girth measurement hourly
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, blood gas (venous/arterial), lactate
  - U+E, creatinine, coagulation screen
  - Blood culture × 1 (peripheral, before antibiotics)
- **Imaging:**
  - **AXR (supine + left lateral decubitus if perforation suspected)** — first-line; look for: pneumatosis intestinalis, portal venous gas, free air, dilated fixed loops
  - **Abdominal US** — second-line; assesses bowel wall thickness, perfusion (Doppler), fluid collections
- **Special tests:** Stool culture if infectious enteritis considered
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **Bell’s Staging (Modified)** — Stage I (suspected): mild distension, bloody stool, non-diagnostic AXR; Stage II (definite): pneumatosis + clinical signs; Stage III (advanced): perforation, peritonitis, shock
- **Never-miss test:** **AXR (erect or cross-table lateral)** to rule out pneumoperitoneum before any surgical decision
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / NICU/ED):**
  - NBM, pass NG tube to free drainage
  - IV fluids: normal saline bolus 10–20 mL/kg if hypotensive (repeat as needed, max 40 mL/kg total, then consider inotropic support)
  - Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics: [check local neonatal sepsis policy] — typically IV benzylpenicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole, or IV piperacillin-tazobactam alone
  - Call paediatric surgery immediately (if any suspicion of perforation/Stage III)
  - Start IV fluids for maintenance: typically 100–150 mL/kg/day glucose 10% + electrolytes (adjust per local protocol)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Continue NBM; TPN via dedicated central line (start within 24h)
  - Daily AXR to monitor progression
  - Strict fluid balance, daily weight, abdominal girth Q4h
  - Trend CRP, lactate, platelets, WCC
  - Repeat blood culture if fever persists
  - Involve neonatology consultant and paediatric surgeon
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Gradual feeding reintroduction after 7–14 days of medical treatment (if no surgery)
  - If surgical: stoma reversal/closure at later date; monitor for short bowel syndrome
  - Neurodevelopmental follow-up (high risk of sequelae)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **NBM** — do not start feeds until clinical and radiological resolution
  - **IV fluids:** maintenance + replacement losses as per local NICU fluid protocol
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** not routinely used in neonates (use LMWH only if specific risk factors; [check local policy])
  - **Observation frequency:** Q1h initially (including NG aspirate, abdominal girth), then Q2h after stability
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **IV piperacillin-tazobactam** [check local policy] — common dose: 80–100 mg/kg/dose Q6–8h (preterm adjusted)
  - **IV gentamicin** — [check local policy]; typical dose: 4–5 mg/kg IV Q24h (monitor trough levels)
  - **IV metronidazole** — 15 mg/kg loading, then 7.5 mg/kg Q12h
- **Common alternatives:** IV meropenem (20 mg/kg Q8–12h) if severe or resistant organisms
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Aminoglycosides: avoid with pre-existing renal impairment; check drug levels before second dose
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam: can cause electrolyte disturbances (Na+ load, K+ loss); monitor U+E
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Gentamicin interval prolonged if renal impairment; dose reduce piperacillin-tazobactam if GFR low; metronidazole dose reduce in severe hepatic disease [check local guidelines]
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Gentamicin — check levels before second dose (trough <2 mg/L); failure to do so risks nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity**; always confirm both patient weight and GFR.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Intestinal perforation leading to peritonitis and septic shock
  - Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)
  - Acute renal failure
  - Necrotising pneumonia (rare)
- **Chronic:**
  - Short bowel syndrome (if significant resection)
  - Intestinal strictures (weeks to months later)
  - Neurodevelopmental delay (especially in infants who required surgery)
  - Cholestatic liver disease (TPN-related)
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality 20–30% (higher if surgical NEC); spontaneous intestinal perforation has lower mortality. Survivors have 30–50% risk of long-term neurodevelopmental issues, especially if severe or preterm <28w.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always get an AXR (erect or cross-table) before calling surgical reg** — if free air, goes directly to theatre
- **Serial CRP and lactate** are best early indicators of progression — a rising CRP >100 mg/L or lactate >4 mmol/L within 24h should prompt urgent surgical review
- **Check NG aspirate colour and volume every hour** — bilious or bloody aspirate indicates ongoing bowel compromise
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT start enteral feeds until clinically and radiologically resolved** — even after 7 days, check with senior
- **Do NOT delay surgical consultation waiting for blood culture results** — clinical deterioration is the trigger
- **Do NOT give routine bowel stimulants (e.g. glycerin suppositories)** — can worsen ischaemia
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening abdominal distension, bloody stools, lethargy, poor feeding, apnoea, temperature >38°C (or <36°C).
- **Follow-up:** Neonatology clinic within 1–2 weeks; if any neurological symptoms, formal neurodevelopmental assessment at 6–12 months.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call paediatric surgery reg immediately if:** AXR shows pneumoperitoneum, abdominal wall erythema/progressive distension, or haemodynamic instability despite fluid bolus.
- **Call NICU consultant if:** Lactate >4 mmol/L, need for inotropes, requirement for >40 mL/kg fluid bolus, or unexplained coagulopathy.
- **Escalate to PICU/NICU consultant if:** GCS <10 (in corrected-age appropriate scale), respiratory failure, or anuria for >6 hours.
- **Use neonatal early warning scores (e.g. NIPE/NEWS adapted) — any single parameter "red" triggers immediate review.**

rawMarkdown:
## Necrotising Enterocolitis (NEC)

**One-liner:** Life-threatening intestinal necrosis in preterm neonates (<32w, <1500g); rapid progression to perforation, peritonitis, septic shock.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Intestinal perforation** → pneumoperitoneum on AXR (free air under diaphragm) + board-like abdomen, septic shock. **Single finding:** abdominal wall erythema and absent bowel sounds with haemodynamic instability.
- **Septic shock due to translocation** → rising lactate, hypotension, mottling, apnoea. **Single finding:** lactate >4 mmol/L despite fluid bolus.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** abdominal distension, feeding intolerance (vomiting, increased gastric residuals), bloody stools, lethargy, temperature instability, apnoea, bradycardia.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Distended, tense, erythematous abdomen
  - Reduced/absent bowel sounds
  - Palpable abdominal mass (in advanced cases)
  - Signs of shock: mottled skin, delayed cap refill, hypotension (late)
  - Perforation: board-like rigidity, extreme tenderness

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Sepsis (non-GI focus)** → positive blood cultures from other source; no bloody stools, normal AXR
- **Spontaneous intestinal perforation (SIP)** → isolated perforation without pneumatosis; more common in ELBW <26w; no pneumatosis on AXR
- **Feeding intolerance (non-NEC)** → mild distension, stable observations, no bloody stools, normal AXR
- **Malrotation with volvulus** → bilious vomiting, distended abdomen, but bloody stools rare; UGI confirms
- **Infectious enteritis** → frequent watery/bloody stools, no pneumatosis on AXR; fever may be present
- **Splitter:** **Pneumatosis intestinalis on AXR** = NEC (not SIP, not simple feeding intolerance)

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - NG tube for decompression + aspirate check
  - Rectal exam for occult blood
  - Observations: HR, RR, SpO2, BP, temperature
  - Abdominal girth measurement hourly
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, blood gas (venous/arterial), lactate
  - U+E, creatinine, coagulation screen
  - Blood culture × 1 (peripheral, before antibiotics)
- **Imaging:**
  - **AXR (supine + left lateral decubitus if perforation suspected)** — first-line; look for: pneumatosis intestinalis, portal venous gas, free air, dilated fixed loops
  - **Abdominal US** — second-line; assesses bowel wall thickness, perfusion (Doppler), fluid collections
- **Special tests:** Stool culture if infectious enteritis considered
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **Bell’s Staging (Modified)** — Stage I (suspected): mild distension, bloody stool, non-diagnostic AXR; Stage II (definite): pneumatosis + clinical signs; Stage III (advanced): perforation, peritonitis, shock
- **Never-miss test:** **AXR (erect or cross-table lateral)** to rule out pneumoperitoneum before any surgical decision

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / NICU/ED):**
  - NBM, pass NG tube to free drainage
  - IV fluids: normal saline bolus 10–20 mL/kg if hypotensive (repeat as needed, max 40 mL/kg total, then consider inotropic support)
  - Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics: [check local neonatal sepsis policy] — typically IV benzylpenicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole, or IV piperacillin-tazobactam alone
  - Call paediatric surgery immediately (if any suspicion of perforation/Stage III)
  - Start IV fluids for maintenance: typically 100–150 mL/kg/day glucose 10% + electrolytes (adjust per local protocol)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Continue NBM; TPN via dedicated central line (start within 24h)
  - Daily AXR to monitor progression
  - Strict fluid balance, daily weight, abdominal girth Q4h
  - Trend CRP, lactate, platelets, WCC
  - Repeat blood culture if fever persists
  - Involve neonatology consultant and paediatric surgeon
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Gradual feeding reintroduction after 7–14 days of medical treatment (if no surgery)
  - If surgical: stoma reversal/closure at later date; monitor for short bowel syndrome
  - Neurodevelopmental follow-up (high risk of sequelae)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **NBM** — do not start feeds until clinical and radiological resolution
  - **IV fluids:** maintenance + replacement losses as per local NICU fluid protocol
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** not routinely used in neonates (use LMWH only if specific risk factors; [check local policy])
  - **Observation frequency:** Q1h initially (including NG aspirate, abdominal girth), then Q2h after stability

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **IV piperacillin-tazobactam** [check local policy] — common dose: 80–100 mg/kg/dose Q6–8h (preterm adjusted)
  - **IV gentamicin** — [check local policy]; typical dose: 4–5 mg/kg IV Q24h (monitor trough levels)
  - **IV metronidazole** — 15 mg/kg loading, then 7.5 mg/kg Q12h
- **Common alternatives:** IV meropenem (20 mg/kg Q8–12h) if severe or resistant organisms
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Aminoglycosides: avoid with pre-existing renal impairment; check drug levels before second dose
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam: can cause electrolyte disturbances (Na+ load, K+ loss); monitor U+E
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Gentamicin interval prolonged if renal impairment; dose reduce piperacillin-tazobactam if GFR low; metronidazole dose reduce in severe hepatic disease [check local guidelines]
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Gentamicin — check levels before second dose (trough <2 mg/L); failure to do so risks nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity**; always confirm both patient weight and GFR.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Intestinal perforation leading to peritonitis and septic shock
  - Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)
  - Acute renal failure
  - Necrotising pneumonia (rare)
- **Chronic:**
  - Short bowel syndrome (if significant resection)
  - Intestinal strictures (weeks to months later)
  - Neurodevelopmental delay (especially in infants who required surgery)
  - Cholestatic liver disease (TPN-related)

### Prognosis
- Mortality 20–30% (higher if surgical NEC); spontaneous intestinal perforation has lower mortality. Survivors have 30–50% risk of long-term neurodevelopmental issues, especially if severe or preterm <28w.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always get an AXR (erect or cross-table) before calling surgical reg** — if free air, goes directly to theatre
- **Serial CRP and lactate** are best early indicators of progression — a rising CRP >100 mg/L or lactate >4 mmol/L within 24h should prompt urgent surgical review
- **Check NG aspirate colour and volume every hour** — bilious or bloody aspirate indicates ongoing bowel compromise

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT start enteral feeds until clinically and radiologically resolved** — even after 7 days, check with senior
- **Do NOT delay surgical consultation waiting for blood culture results** — clinical deterioration is the trigger
- **Do NOT give routine bowel stimulants (e.g. glycerin suppositories)** — can worsen ischaemia

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening abdominal distension, bloody stools, lethargy, poor feeding, apnoea, temperature >38°C (or <36°C).
- **Follow-up:** Neonatology clinic within 1–2 weeks; if any neurological symptoms, formal neurodevelopmental assessment at 6–12 months.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call paediatric surgery reg immediately if:** AXR shows pneumoperitoneum, abdominal wall erythema/progressive distension, or haemodynamic instability despite fluid bolus.
- **Call NICU consultant if:** Lactate >4 mmol/L, need for inotropes, requirement for >40 mL/kg fluid bolus, or unexplained coagulopathy.
- **Escalate to PICU/NICU consultant if:** GCS <10 (in corrected-age appropriate scale), respiratory failure, or anuria for >6 hours.
- **Use neonatal early warning scores (e.g. NIPE/NEWS adapted) — any single parameter "red" triggers immediate review.**
